stories.title: Singing mailman serenades woman who lost her husband, and people rally to get him a life-changing gift
stories.summary: Mailman Lavonte Harvey's daily singing brought joy to a widow who'd lost her husband of 50 years. When her granddaughter filmed his birthday serenade, the video hit 14 million views—and strangers raised enough money to buy him a truck, cutting his brutal 2.5-hour commute to 30 minutes.
stories.extended_summary: Lavonte Harvey spent five hours a day commuting to deliver mail and spread joy through song. For one widow who'd just lost her husband of 50 years, his voice became her "daily dose of life." When her granddaughter asked him to sing for her grandmother's birthday, the moment went viral with 14 million views. Viewers learned about Harvey's exhausting commute and rallied on GoFundMe to buy him a truck. Now he can walk his dog in the morning, feed him on time, and finally have a life outside work.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)

stories.title: From addiction to redemption: Virginia missionary’s recovery ministry leads to new church
stories.summary: Fred and Casey Weymouth went from living in the backseat of a car and struggling with addiction to founding The Fix, a residential recovery program on 30 acres in rural Virginia. The ministry helps people battling drugs, alcohol, and homelessness find healing through faith — the same path that saved the couple's lives.
stories.extended_summary: The ministry sits at the end of a bumpy dirt road, 46 miles from the nearest city, and that distance is the point. Fred and Casey used to sleep in Casey's car, always chasing the next fix. They tried every 12-step program they could find, but nothing worked until they found Jesus. Now they've turned their story into a lifeline for thirty people who live on the property, learning that recovery isn't just about stopping — it's about being remade from the inside out. The name is a deliberate echo of their past: what used to mean their next high now means the person who saved them.

stories.title: Black Mom Survives Heart Attack, Credits Her Daughter for Saving Her Life
stories.summary: When 15-year-old Mahogany Milton's mom collapsed at home, stopped breathing, and started turning blue, she didn't freeze—she started CPR. Her mother, B'Lon Calloway, had a 99% artery blockage and was minutes from death. Mahogany's training from the American Heart Association kept her alive until paramedics arrived.
stories.extended_summary: B'Lon Calloway came home from work feeling unwell, took one big breath, and stopped breathing. Her daughter Mahogany watched her turn blue and cold. At 15, Mahogany had learned CPR from both her mother and the American Heart Association's STEM Goes Red program. She started compressions while talking to 911, praying her mom would survive. Doctors later found a 99% blockage in B'Lon's left anterior descending artery—she'd had a heart attack and cardiac arrest. But because Mahogany acted fast, her mom is alive. "God saw a system to let me make it home," B'Lon said through tears. "Even if it was just for her."

stories.title: Dozens of Strangers Form Parade for Man with Cancer, Driving Classic Cars Passed his Home for One Last Surprise (WATCH)
stories.summary: When Max Archuleta's granddaughter posted on social media that her 80-year-old grandfather—diagnosed with terminal cancer—could no longer attend his beloved car shows, she hoped a few vintage cars might drive by. Instead, 50 classic car owners lined up in Lakewood, Colorado, honking and waving as they paraded past his home.
stories.extended_summary: Max Archuleta spent his life loving classic cars, attending shows whenever he could. But at 80, with terminal cancer draining his energy, those days were over. His granddaughter Annaliesse posted a request on social media, hoping a handful of car owners might drive by their Lakewood home. She expected maybe five. Around 50 showed up—1951 Ford Shoeboxes, cars in every color, some with American flags, one with Max's photo taped to the side. They lined up in single file, honking their horns and waving as they drove slowly past. "I couldn't believe everybody," Max said. "Just all this for me."

stories.title: Foley 11-Year-Old Turns Heartbreak Into Hope
stories.summary: When 4-year-old Gardner was diagnosed with leukemia, his 11-year-old sister Mary Grace turned grief into action. She designed hopeful T-shirts for him to wear during treatment, then committed herself to learning everything she can about finding a cure — because losing her best friend isn't an option.
stories.extended_summary: Two weeks after the diagnosis, while the reality of B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia was still settling over their Foley, Alabama home, Mary Grace made a promise that now drives everything she does: "I'm going to help find a cure." Her little brother Gardner is more than family — he's the one who dances in the living room and sings without hesitation, the one who calls her "Mary Grace the Amazing." At first, love showed up as bright T-shirts covered in messages of hope, armor made of encouragement for the scariest days. But fear is transforming into something stronger now, something that looks like a future scientist who refuses to let her best friend's joy be taken away.

stories.title: Feeding the Blizzard
stories.summary: For 15 years, Sandy Bessingpas has opened her rural Minnesota home to young hockey players far from their families. After her husband died and her sons moved away, she filled her empty bedrooms with Blizzard Hockey team members—nearly 60 players over the years, each welcomed with home-cooked meals and a supportive place to belong.
stories.extended_summary: Sandy Bessingpas answered a newspaper ad 15 years ago, naively offering to host hockey players in her Holmes City farmhouse. She was used to boys—she'd raised two sons with her late husband, a military veteran. After he died in 2006 and her sons moved away, the house felt too quiet for someone who "always likes to have people around" and loves to cook. So she kept answering the call. Nearly 60 Blizzard Hockey players have lived with her over the years, coming from Colorado, Wisconsin, and beyond, finding warmth, home-cooked meals, and a surrogate family in the Minnesota woods.

stories.title: Delafield kids honored for saving their grandfather's life
stories.summary: When their grandfather collapsed from cardiac arrest, 8-year-old Everett and 6-year-old Annemarie called 911, then ran outside to redirect police who arrived at the wrong house. Their quick actions saved his life, earning them letters of commendation and challenge coins from the Delafield Police Department.
stories.extended_summary: Jeff was reading his grandkids a bedtime story when everything went blank. His heart stopped, and he collapsed on the floor. Everett, 8, and Annemarie, 6, were terrified — "I thought I was going to collapse of scaredness," Everett said — but they didn't freeze. They called 911 and their mom, who was fifteen minutes away. When police arrived at the wrong address, the kids ran outside in the January cold and redirected them. Jeff was rushed to the hospital, where doctors inserted a pacemaker. He spent four days recovering. Chief Landon Nyren said the outcome could have been very different without the children's exceptional awareness and courage.

stories.title: At Camp No Limits, disabilities hold no one back from a ride down Saddleback
stories.summary: Camp No Limits brings children with limb differences to Saddleback Ski Area, where they learn to ski and snowboard under one-on-one mentorship from adults with similar conditions. The program, run by volunteers who grew up with disabilities themselves, teaches kids that there's a place for them in sports through adaptive equipment and personalized instruction.
stories.extended_summary: Families from across Maine gather at Saddleback for a weekend that opens up a world many of these children thought was closed to them. Some kids glide solo down the slopes, others are tethered to mentors, and some carve turns from adaptive monoskis — but all of them are learning that their bodies don't determine what's possible. David Harrell, who was born missing his right hand, started volunteering twelve years ago because he wished a younger version of himself could have experienced this. Operations director Kelsey Moody says it plainly: in a state like Maine, these kids need to be able to love the outdoors, and this weekend proves they can.

stories.title: How Deciding to Donate a Kidney Saved My Own Life
stories.summary: When the author applied to donate a kidney to a stranger through the National Kidney Registry, they hoped to improve someone else's life. Instead, the screening process caught a life-threatening health issue in their own body—one they never would have found otherwise. Deciding to be a donor ended up saving their own life.
stories.extended_summary: The signs kept appearing: intersection posters asking people to register for kidney donation, a man on the roadside with a "NEED KIDNEY 4 WIFE" sandwich board, a documentary about altruistic donors. The author had considered donating years earlier for an aunt with kidney disease, and the idea wouldn't let go. So they applied to the National Kidney Registry to donate to a stranger. During the screening process, doctors found a serious, hidden health problem—one that could have gone undetected and been fatal. The author had set out to save someone else's life and ended up saving their own.

stories.title: Sharing life on the streets to sharing a stage: Mother and son defeat the odds and return to college
stories.summary: Herica Paniagua lost her teenage son to suicide, spiraled into 15 years of addiction and homelessness, and nearly disappeared. But her youngest son—the seven-year-old who found his brother's body—never stopped believing in her. This spring, they'll walk across the stage together at Long Beach City College, both graduating.
stories.extended_summary: Herica Paniagua was a certified drug and alcohol counselor, a single mom working and studying to give her four kids a better life. Then her 17-year-old son died by suicide. His seven-year-old brother, Edrick Salgado, was the one who found him. The trauma broke everything. Paniagua fell into addiction, lost stable housing, and spent more than a decade on the streets. But Salgado never gave up on her. Now 22, he's graduating from Long Beach City College—and so is his mom. They'll walk the stage together this spring, proof that even the deepest loss doesn't have to be the end of the story.

stories.title: Black Man Gets Kidney Transplant from Brother After 8 Years on Dialysis
stories.summary: After eight years on dialysis, Keith Preston received a kidney from his older brother Willis. The successful transplant gave Keith a second chance at life and inspired both brothers to advocate for organ donation, particularly within the African American community.
stories.extended_summary: Keith spent eight years choking on fluid buildup, sleeping upright because lying down felt like death. By 2024, his remaining kidney was functioning at just 14 percent. He asked his older brother Willis for help, and despite fears about his own health — "What'll happen if the other one goes bad?" — Willis went through months of testing at the University of Mississippi Medical Center. In August 2025, they learned they were a match. On February 17, they underwent a ten-hour transplant surgery. Both brothers are now recovering, and the experience has transformed them into advocates. They've partnered with the Mississippi Organ Recovery Agency, with Willis planning outreach events focused on kidney health in Black communities.

stories.title: A Fire Inside: Merced Firefighter Rhett Avant Returns to Duty After Life-Changing Crash
stories.summary: Merced firefighter Rhett Avant returned to full duty ten months after a motorcycle crash that mangled his foot. Before help arrived, he tourniqueted his own leg, called 911, gave dispatchers his location, and phoned his fire crew — all while bleeding on the roadside.
stories.extended_summary: April 18, 2025 was Good Friday, and Rhett Avant was on his motorcycle when he clipped the rear dual of a semi-trailer. The impact flipped him and left him sprawled in the middle of the road with a mangled foot and blood pooling beneath him. What happened next stops people in their tracks: he butt-scooted himself to the shoulder so he wouldn't get hit again, directed bystanders to his medical kit, applied his own tourniquet, took over the 911 call, and notified the Merced Fire Chief that one of their own was down. On February 18, 2026, he walked back into the bay for the first time as a full-duty firefighter, looked at his gear on the rig, and thought: "This is awesome. I'm actually doing this."

stories.title: Good Samaritans Sprang into Action to Save Baby That Fell into Lake Michigan
stories.summary: A fierce gust of wind at Chicago's Belmont Harbor caught a baby's bassinet and blew it—cradle and all—into Lake Michigan. Lio Cundiff jumped in without a plan, holding the stroller above water while an airline pilot tossed him a Cubs jacket to keep him afloat. Together they pulled the baby out alive.
stories.extended_summary: Lio Cundiff was on the phone with his aunt when he heard a bloodcurdling scream. A gust of wind had caught a stroller's bassinet and sent it tumbling into Lake Michigan, baby inside. The nanny was in too much panic to act. "I guess I'm going in," Cundiff thought, and jumped. He couldn't get the baby out, so he lifted the top half of the soaking stroller out of the water to keep it from sinking. Enter Luis Kapost, an American Airlines pilot, who threw one arm of his Cubs jacket to Cundiff and held the other, keeping him afloat until help arrived. First responders rushed both man and baby to the hospital. Both survived.

stories.title: UPSIDE: Pastors now ‘twin brothers’ after kidney donation
stories.summary: Two Filipino American priests in San Francisco have known each other for nearly 30 years, connected by faith and heritage. This January, their bond deepened in an unprecedented way: Rev. Francis Mark Garbo donated a kidney to Rev. Raymund Reyes, becoming what church authorities believe is the first organ donation between two priests in the Archdiocese.
stories.extended_summary: Rev. Raymund Reyes was diagnosed with stage 3 chronic kidney disease in 2019, the same year he became pastor of St. Augustine Catholic Church in South San Francisco. As his condition worsened, he faced dialysis three times a week—or finding a living donor. Enter Rev. Francis Mark Garbo, pastor of Mission Dolores Basilica, a friend of nearly 30 years. Twenty days into 2025, Garbo gave Reyes a kidney. "Father Francis is a saint, my hero and now my twin brother," Reyes said. Church authorities believe it's the first organ donation between two priests in the Archdiocese of San Francisco—a story of faith, hope, and generosity.

stories.title: Black Woman Therapist Defeats DoorDash in Federal Trademark Battle — Without a Lawyer
stories.summary: Dr. Ashley Bryant, a Black therapist and entrepreneur, defended her trademark against DoorDash in federal court — without hiring a lawyer. She won. The decision allows her mental health software company, LeadDash, to keep its name and proceed to full federal registration, permanently blocking the $70-billion tech giant from challenging it again.
stories.extended_summary: DoorDash filed opposition in June 2025, claiming that LeadDash — Dr. Bryant's EHR and marketing platform built for therapists — would confuse consumers with its family of "DASH" products. It was a David-and-Goliath matchup with impossible odds: a Black woman entrepreneur from Edmond, Oklahoma, a licensed counselor with a PhD and a mission to help mental health professionals grow their practices, facing down one of America's biggest tech companies. She represented herself. On February 18, 2026, the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board dismissed DoorDash's opposition with prejudice — meaning it's over, permanently, and Dr. Bryant's brand will receive full federal protection. She built something to heal people, and she wasn't going to let anyone take the name she built it under.

stories.title: Okizu strong: How NorCal camp helps kids battling cancer through power of community
stories.summary: Six-year-old Mabel Wong went from picking cherries and practicing judo to battling acute myeloid leukemia, a rare blood cancer. Her family found support through Okizu, a Northern California nonprofit that's been helping kids with cancer and their families heal through community since 1982—proving no child has to face this fight alone.
stories.extended_summary: Mabel Wong was always moving—agility courses, judo, cherry picking in the wild. Then exhaustion hit. Her parents thought it was a growth spurt, maybe not enough sleep. Then her eyes swelled at school spirit day. Five days and a fever later, the diagnosis came: acute myeloid leukemia, a rare blood cancer. "I really felt like I floated out of my own body," her father Tim said. That's when they found Okizu, a NorCal nonprofit that's been supporting kids with cancer and their families since 1982. Through camp and community, families learn they don't have to face this alone. They call it "Okizu strong."

stories.title: New Orleans brings back the house call, sending nurses to visit newborns and moms
stories.summary: New Orleans is reviving old-fashioned house calls for new parents, sending nurses directly to homes to check on moms and newborns. The program catches health issues early and eases the overwhelm of early parenting—no packing up a screaming baby for a clinic trip. It's especially vital in Louisiana, one of the nation's worst states for maternal and infant health.
stories.extended_summary: Lisa Bonfield was exhausted, hadn't figured out the infant car seat, and had a million questions she'd never even thought to ask. Then a nurse showed up at her door when baby Adele was just weeks old. New Orleans is bringing back house calls for new parents through Family Connects, a program that sends registered nurses to check on every city resident who's recently given birth—up to three visits, no questions asked. In a state with some of the worst maternal and infant health outcomes in the nation, catching problems early and giving hands-on support can save lives. It's an old-fashioned solution to a modern crisis.

stories.title: The West Philly club teaching boys to be gentlemen
stories.summary: Dwayne Eric Frazier, a West Philadelphia teacher, retired firefighter, and reverend, created the Distinguished Young Gentlemen club where boys suit up, learn confidence, and practice skills like maintaining eye contact and firm handshakes. What started as an after-school program is now a registered nonprofit transforming lives beyond the classroom.
stories.extended_summary: Every other week, more than two dozen boys walk into Lewis C. Cassidy Academics Plus School wearing suits and neckties, many for the first time in their lives. One student says it plainly: "It feels good. I think I look handsome in it." Behind this transformation is Dwayne Eric Frazier, who grew up in North Philly learning from his father how to tie a tie, polish shoes, and look people in the eye. Those lessons followed him through military service and 26 years with the Philadelphia Fire Department. Now he's passing them forward through the Distinguished Young Gentlemen club, teaching boys that confidence isn't just about appearance — it's about carrying yourself like you matter, because you do.

stories.title: Hot air balloon rescue 900 feet in the air
stories.summary: Firefighters in Longview, Texas pulled off a dramatic high-angle rescue after a hot air balloon snagged on a cell tower nearly 1,000 feet in the air, leaving two passengers clinging to the swinging basket. Using multiple 300-foot ropes, first responders lowered the terrified pair safely to the ground.
stories.extended_summary: The rainbow balloon basket swung hard in the wind, Lieutenant Steve Winchell reported, while two passengers gripped the edges for dear life, dangling almost a thousand feet above Texas ground. Drone footage captured the heart-stopping scene: a colorful balloon tangled in metal lattice, first responders threading ropes down from the tower, and two people who had gone up for a peaceful ride now frozen in place, waiting to be saved. Firefighters worked rope by rope, 300 feet at a time, until both passengers were back on solid ground. It's the kind of rescue that makes you rethink ever leaving the earth's surface.

stories.title: They came back to plant what the flood took away
stories.summary: After last year's catastrophic July 4th flooding killed 117 people and scarred the Guadalupe River, volunteers and biologists spent months clearing debris and have now begun planting thousands of native tree cuttings along the banks. The restoration work marks a turning point in healing the river and the community that depends on it.
stories.extended_summary: The flood hit Kerrville on July 4, 2025, claiming 117 lives and leaving the Guadalupe River choked with debris and broken trees. Recovery has been slow, measured in cleared banks and quiet determination. Now, Texas Parks and Wildlife biologist Gaby Tamez is leading volunteers in planting over 2,000 native cuttings — not typical nursery trees with roots, but bare stems that will take hold in the riverbank and grow. It's far fewer than what the flood took, but it's a strong start, and it represents something bigger: a community choosing to rebuild what was lost, one cutting at a time, stabilizing the river and restoring the beauty that defined this place.

stories.title: Providing an option for home births: Midwife opens practice, providing care in a ‘maternity care desert’
stories.summary: Nikki Meyer, a mother of five who had four home births, opened Deep Wells Midwifery in Wells to serve expectant mothers in what's known as a "maternity care desert." As a certified professional midwife, she provides full prenatal care in her office and attends home births—giving rural families an option when hospitals scale back labor and delivery services.
stories.extended_summary: When rural hospitals pull or scale back labor and delivery services, expectant mothers are left asking: what are our options? Nikki Meyer opened Deep Wells Midwifery in Wells in early 2024 to answer that question. As a mother of five who had four of her own children at home, she knows the power of choice. Now, as a certified professional midwife, she provides prenatal care on the same schedule as an OBGYN, conducting exams and routine lab work in her office. She attends home births and orders ultrasounds to providers of the mother's choice. In what's known as a "maternity care desert," Meyer gives families an alternative rooted in personal care and experience.

stories.title: Treacherous 43-Day Mountain Search Finally Reunites Dog With Owner After it Bolted During Road Trip: ‘It was amazing’
stories.summary: Steven Maa's dog Rocky bolted into Colorado's Rocky Mountains during a blizzard, forcing Steven to continue to his new California job after days of searching. Summit Lost Pet Rescue took over, setting up trail cams and scent stations. After 43 treacherous days at 10,000 feet elevation, Rocky was finally found and reunited with his grateful owner.
stories.extended_summary: Rocky appeared on the trail cam, then vanished again. Steven Maa had already spent multiple 16-hour days searching Colorado's mountains at nearly 10,000 feet, but his new job in California couldn't wait any longer. He had to leave his dog behind, heartbroken, while a blizzard rolled in and temperatures plunged below zero. Summit Lost Pet Rescue—a nonprofit with over 200 recoveries last year—refused to give up. They kept cameras running, maintained scent stations with Steven's clothes and a dog bed, and launched social media campaigns. Forty-three days after Rocky bolted from a pet sitter, the mottled brown-and-black pooch was finally recovered and reunited with Steven.

stories.title: Benefit at Bakersfield School raises $45,000 for local mother with cancer
stories.summary: Bakersfield, population 186, filled a room with over 200 people for a benefit auction supporting Natasha Crites, who's been battling breast cancer and life-threatening gallbladder complications. Neighbors scraped chili bowls clean, raised bidding cards, and opened wallets—raising $45,000 for their beloved neighbor who hasn't been able to work since last summer.
stories.extended_summary: In a town of 186 residents, more than 200 people showed up. Bakersfield's grassroots generosity was on full display at a Feb. 7 benefit auction and chili supper for Natasha Crites, who's been fighting breast cancer along with life-threatening gallbladder complications that have kept her from working since last summer. Neighbors spent weeks organizing, soliciting donations from local businesses, and gathering items to auction. When the night came, the room overflowed with people scraping chili bowls clean and raising bidding cards. The result: $45,000 raised. Organizers say local businesses "never hesitate" to donate to benefits—a testament to a community that shows up.
stories.display_reason: medium image not worth the space

stories.title: 'What a privilege': Artist sketches portraits of nursing home residents
stories.summary: Artist Scott Gunvaldson sketched portraits of 10 Pioneer Care nursing home residents as part of "Reflections of a Generation." When 91-year-old Sally Christenson saw her portrait, she laughed: "I can't believe it. He created something out of nothing." After the exhibit, each sketch goes home with the resident who modeled for it.
stories.extended_summary: Sally Christenson, 91, sat in a chair while artist Scott Gunvaldson worked. "He created something out of nothing," she laughed when she saw her portrait capturing nine decades of life. Ten residents of Pioneer Care in Fergus Falls became models for "Reflections of a Generation," an art exhibit made possible by the Lake Region Arts Council. Lars Larson, a 94-year-old retired farmer, studied his sketch closely: "I think it is pretty darn close, I really do." When the exhibit ends, each portrait goes home with the resident, a permanent reminder that their faces and stories matter.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: Lafayette Soccer Coach Exemplifies What True Sportsmanship Is
stories.summary: A Lafayette youth soccer coach stopped a lopsided game to let every player on the losing team score a goal, with the winning team cheering for each one. Coach Underwood's act of sportsmanship lifted the spirits of defeated young players and showed parents what youth sports should be about.
stories.extended_summary: The scoreboard told the story: one team was dominating, and the younger girls on the other side looked crushed. Their shoulders sagged, their faces said it all — they wanted out. That's when Coach Underwood stopped the game with minutes left, lined up every kid on the losing team, and told them each to kick the ball into the goal. His own players stood behind the net, and when each opposing player scored, they erupted in cheers. Parents in the stands joined in. It was a moment that flipped the script on youth sports — no yelling at refs, no parent drama, just one coach deciding that lifting kids up mattered more than the final score.

stories.title: ‘Miracle Matt’ Reunites With Orlando Firefighters Nearly 12 Years After Life-Saving Rescue
stories.summary: Orlando firefighters reunited with Matthew "Miracle Matt" Manzari nearly 12 years after rescuing him from a devastating electrical accident that responders say rarely ends in survival. Manzari has undergone over 80 surgeries, returned to wake-skating, travels as a motivational speaker, and now has three children—the first was on the way the day of the accident.
stories.extended_summary: Matthew Manzari struck a power line while trimming trees near an Orlando church on June 25, 2014. The electrical shock was the kind that rarely ends in survival—severe burns, traumatic injuries across his body, but somehow he remained conscious. Orlando Firehouse 3 arrived within minutes, stabilized him, and got him to the hospital. Nearly 12 years later, they reunited Wednesday with the man they call "Miracle Matt." He's had over 80 surgeries since, returned to wake-skating, and now travels the country as a motivational speaker. His wife was pregnant with their first child the day of the accident; they've since had two more. The crew reflected on the calm, coordinated response that gave him his fighting chance.

stories.title: ‘It’s beautiful to see’: Milwaukee youths help transform vacant 53206 lot into a community garden
stories.summary: Milwaukee teens with We Got This are converting a vacant corner lot in the 53206 ZIP code into Coaches Corner Garden, despite initial fears about neighborhood violence. The project is part of their work transforming over 15 vacant lots into community gardens where they grow free produce and flowers for residents while building job skills.
stories.extended_summary: Student leaders Nariah Eubanks, 17, and Denzil Brinson, 16, were scared at first. Violence had broken out months earlier on the vacant corner lot they were asked to transform. They hesitated, unsure if it was safe to work there. But then they realized: if they showed fear, everyone else would stay scared too. So they moved forward, breaking ground on Coaches Corner Garden in Milwaukee's 53206 ZIP code. It's one of 15 formerly vacant lots the teens maintain through We Got This, planting produce and flowers to give away free while earning leadership skills and paychecks. The corner opens in early June.

stories.title: With the traditional lion dance, this family shares Chinese culture across Maine
stories.summary: Steve Wong has been performing traditional Chinese lion dances across Maine for over three decades, turning what started as a solo school visit into a multi-generational family tradition. His son and grandchildren now join him, sharing Chinese culture at schools, weddings, and cultural events statewide.
stories.extended_summary: When a Bangor-area school called Sing's Polynesian Restaurant in the early 1990s asking for someone to demonstrate Chinese lion dancing, Steve Wong borrowed his aunt's costume and said yes. Students stared at the lion's fluttering eyes, painted mouth, and shimmering fur-trimmed body — mesmerized. That single performance turned into 30-plus years of sharing Chinese culture across Maine. Now 76, Wong performs alongside his son Rodger, 41, and two grandchildren at schools, weddings, restaurant openings, and cultural festivals. Inside the papier-mâché head, Rodger nods to make the ears flap and pulls red strings to blink the lion's pink eyes — a family tradition passed down one dance at a time.

stories.title: Morning Light, Lifeline for Neighbors: Englewood Meals on Wheels at the Heart of the Community
stories.summary: At Englewood's Meals on Wheels, director Kathleen Macklin oversees a volunteer-powered operation that delivers far more than food. From cold coolers packed with fresh fruit to weekly bread loaves and oversized muffins, every detail is designed to ensure no one in the community goes hungry between deliveries.
stories.extended_summary: The volunteers arrive early, greeted by Kathleen Macklin's welcoming smile at the headquarters of Meals on Wheels in Englewood. Against one wall, cold coolers hum as staff pack fresh fruit cups. Nearby, stacks of bread loaves reach toward the ceiling—each client gets a loaf every week. Today's bonus: golden, oversized muffins that arrive alongside hot meals being prepared in the kitchen. For Macklin, this bustling room isn't just logistics—it's where simple acts of packing bags transform into lifelines for neighbors who need them most.

stories.title: Lubbock’s Alzheimer’s Football League turns powder-puff game into push for research and care
stories.summary: Lubbock's Alzheimer's Football League raises funds through a women's powder-puff game, aiming for $100,000 this year — more than double last year's $40,000 goal. Co-chair Jessica Stafford, whose grandmother battled Alzheimer's for a decade, partners with Garrison Institute on Aging to support research and caregivers.
stories.extended_summary: Jessica Stafford watched her grandmother battle Alzheimer's for over a decade, and that's why she's co-chairing Lubbock's Alzheimer's Football League — a community effort that drafts teams, holds scrimmages, and culminates in a women's powder-puff game at Jones AT&T Stadium. This year, they're swinging for $100,000, more than doubling last year's $40,000 goal. The committee itself doubled in size to 20 members, and draft day pulled the community together in a way that felt rare. Funds go to Garrison Institute on Aging, which runs a brain bank and connects caregivers with real support — because it's wild that in 2026, so little is still known about this disease.

stories.title: Take a Breath: Sheriff’s chaplain provides more than religion
stories.summary: After 25 years as a full-time pastor, Jeff Humphrey needed to regroup. He moved to Whidbey Island and started a sign business, but his calling found him again when he became Senior Chaplain for Island County Sheriff's Office and North Whidbey Fire and Rescue, serving crime victims, families, and stressed first responders at all hours.
stories.extended_summary: Jeff Humphrey spent 25 years giving his congregation everything he had. He won't say he burned out, but by 2012 he needed to regroup. He and his wife Dawn moved to Whidbey Island, started Humphrey Signs, and figured his pastoral days were behind him. Then the Senior Chaplain role with Island County Sheriff's Office and North Whidbey Fire and Rescue came along—and "just lit me on fire," he says. Now he answers calls in the middle of the night, supports crime victims and grieving families, and counsels first responders through their own stress. It's a big job with no predictable hours, but Humphrey found his calling again: serving the community, just in a different uniform.

stories.title: United Airlines employees donate 15,000 Pokemon Cards to boy who lost collection in Denver
stories.summary: Seven-year-old Reid lost his beloved Pokémon card collection at Denver International Airport. When his dad posted about it online, United Airlines employees from Hawaii to Ireland raided their personal collections and sent cards to Denver. Reid received over 15,000 cards weighing 90 pounds—and he's already hosted a trading party, sending each classmate home with 60 cards.
stories.extended_summary: Airport security won't catch everything—including a seven-year-old's Pokémon binder making a break for it at Denver International Airport. When Reid's collection vanished, his father Graham took to social media. United Airlines' social team spotted the post and asked: How can we help? The answer came from United employees worldwide who apparently take "gotta catch 'em all" seriously. Workers from Hawaii to Ireland raided their personal collections, sending more than 15,000 cards weighing roughly 90 pounds to Denver. Reid and his family collected the haul in person, but he's not hoarding it—he's already hosted a trading party, sending each classmate home with 60 cards and planning to donate even more.

stories.title: Wakulla Senior Center crochet class donates handmade blankets and hats to newborns in the county
stories.summary: A crocheting class at Wakulla Senior Center is making handmade blankets and hats for every newborn in the county. The Wakulla Pregnancy Center gives each new mother a blanket when she has a positive pregnancy test—a tangible first gift symbolizing the start of a new journey. For one mother who'd tried for years to conceive, receiving that blanket was deeply emotional.
stories.extended_summary: For one mother who'd tried for years to get pregnant, receiving a handmade crocheted blanket at her positive pregnancy test was overwhelming. "This is a baby. This is real. I'm gonna have this baby," she realized, holding something tangible for the first time. Women in a crocheting class at Wakulla Senior Center donate these blankets and hats to the Wakulla Pregnancy Center in Crawfordville. Class leader Eileen Bramblett says they want to give young families—who often don't have much—something comforting that babies can keep for life. The donations are also a way of paying forward kindness the seniors once received themselves.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: From Legacy to Lifeline: Serenity Home Aims to Transform the Futures of Birmingham’s Teen Girls
stories.summary: Tomeka Mills is building Birmingham's first purpose-built group home for displaced teen girls, honoring her late father's dream of helping people in need. After learning that only 17% of foster girls aged 11-16 get adopted, she founded A.W. Brooks Serenity Home to give these overlooked teens a safe, stable place to call home.
stories.extended_summary: Every corner in Birmingham had a sign calling for foster parents. Tomeka Mills was shocked by how many there were during a drive with her daughter, a social worker. Then came the statistic that changed everything: only 17% of girls aged 11-16 in foster care ever get adopted. Mills thought of all the girls in her Ensley community who needed safe places to stay. Her late father, Reverend Arthur W. Brooks, had dreamed of helping displaced people but never saw it fulfilled. Now Mills is bringing his vision to life—building the first purpose-built group home for displaced teen girls in the Birmingham area.

stories.title: Years After Traumatic Snowboarding Accident, Survivor Makes Strides With Brain-Focused Workouts
stories.summary: Thomas Ruble, who suffered a traumatic brain injury in a 2013 snowboarding accident, is making new progress after years of plateaued recovery. Working with neuro-specialist Carolayn Crane at Pyour Core Pilates, he's defying the two-year recovery limit doctors typically cite for TBI patients.
stories.extended_summary: Thomas Ruble — T Slice to his friends — hit his head snowboarding on Hunter Mountain in 2013 at age 16, leaving him with a traumatic brain injury and fully dependent on a wheelchair. For over a decade, his family tried everything: physical therapy, occupational therapy, all of it. But they'd plateaued. Doctors say two years post-TBI is where recovery stops. Then his sister, studying occupational therapy, connected him with Carolayn Crane, a neuro-specialist and Pilates instructor. Thomas and Crane clicked immediately, and he's now making strides no one expected — proving that "wherever you are is where you'll stay" doesn't have to be true.

stories.title: The Divorce Doula: Chantay Sanders helps women turn endings into launchpads
stories.summary: Chantay Sanders, a Creative Living Strategist with an NYU MBA, helps women navigate divorce as a certified "Divorce Doula." Drawing from her experience supporting a friend through divorce 15 years ago, she now provides faith-based coaching through what she calls the "labor of letting go."
stories.extended_summary: Chantay Sanders calls herself a Divorce Doula because women in crisis need the same kind of support during the labor of letting go as they do during childbirth. Fifteen years ago, she guided a close friend through a devastating divorce without realizing she was coaching — she was just showing up. Now she's turned that instinct into a calling, combining an NYU Stern MBA with double certifications in life, divorce, and business coaching. For women of faith especially, divorce can trigger an identity crisis and a faith crisis at once: you've been praying, and what you thought would happen doesn't.

stories.title: ‘I want to be someone who people can count on': Woman aims to serve the Karen community in any way she can
stories.summary: Kmwe Win works for Albert Lea schools as a success coach, interpreter, and Adult Basic Education staff, but her real impact comes from being on call 24/7 for her fellow Karen community members. Whether it's 2 a.m. interpreting or helping fix something broken, Win shows up, driven by her belief that serving others is what life is about.
stories.extended_summary: It's 2 a.m. and Kmwe Win's phone rings. Someone in Albert Lea's Karen community needs help—maybe interpreting, maybe filling out paperwork, maybe fixing something that broke. Win gets up and goes. By day she works for Albert Lea schools as a success coach, interpreter, and Adult Basic Education staff, helping people apply for jobs and assistance. On weekends she's a youth leader at Zion Karen Baptist Church. But the help never stops. "Whenever they need me," she says simply. Win moved to Albert Lea in 2012, graduated high school in 2017, and has spent every year since proving one belief: "Serving others makes me happy. I want to be someone who people can count on."

stories.title: Unified students have their day
stories.summary: Cloquet High School's unified PE program, which combines general and special education students, earned national banner recognition from the Special Olympics in November. At a pep rally celebrating the honor, the unified team beat a staff team 10-2 in an exhibition basketball game while the crowd erupted in support.
stories.extended_summary: The crowd erupted every time a unified student scored or stole the ball from a teacher. When the staff team scored their lone basket, the reaction was tepid. Cloquet High School's gym was packed last week for a pep rally celebrating something rare: national banner recognition from the Special Olympics for the school's unified PE program, which combines general and special education students. The unified team dominated an exhibition basketball game against teachers and staff, winning 10-2 while excitable high schoolers cheered them on. The program meets daily and competes in regional sports, but it's about more than athletics—it's about creating opportunities for students to do things together, breaking down barriers one game at a time.

stories.title: Benito, a raccoon rescued by South Philly neighbors during cold spell, is on the mend at a busy wildlife center
stories.summary: A severely injured raccoon named Benito, rescued by South Philly neighbors during February's brutal cold snap, is recovering at Raven Ridge Wildlife Center. After 10 days of community effort, the emaciated raccoon with mange and frostbite was finally captured. His fur is now growing back and he's reached a healthy weight.
stories.extended_summary: Benito had no fur, frostbite covering his body, and open wounds — neighbors couldn't understand how he survived 10 days in Philadelphia's longest cold snap in over two decades. The community on South Cleveland Street refused to give up, setting out food daily until the dying raccoon finally took the bait. At Raven Ridge Wildlife Center, the thick crust covering his skin has fallen off after warm medicated baths. His fur is sprouting back, and the raccoon who once stunk and looked beyond saving has reached a healthy weight.

stories.title: Local women launch Prom Closet to make Prom affordable for area girls
stories.summary: Two local women launched a Prom Closet to help high school girls afford prom without financial stress. Kalie Hanisch, who runs a small boutique, partnered with Traci Sees to donate her stored dresses and create a free resource for area students.
stories.extended_summary: Kalie Hanisch had a storage unit full of boutique dresses just sitting there, and she knew exactly what to do with them. When she realized no one locally was running a prom closet this year, she teamed up with Traci Sees to make sure every high school girl could find something beautiful to wear — at zero cost. With prom season approaching, they're giving girls access to formal dresses they'd otherwise never be able to afford, removing the financial barrier that can make prom feel out of reach.

stories.title: A Night to Shine on the red carpet
stories.summary: Night to Shine in Richmond Hill hosts a prom for people with special needs, complete with red carpet, donated formalwear, and volunteers who return year after year. For residents at Bryan County Health & Rehab who attend, it's the only time all year they leave the facility.
stories.extended_summary: Jessica Warren sat still while a family friend brushed her hair, a blue sequined dress waiting nearby. In a few hours, she'd walk a red carpet at Night to Shine — Richmond Hill's prom for people with special needs, age 14 and up. Weeks before, families picked from 600 donated dresses and 200 suits at no cost, removing every financial barrier. At Bryan County Health & Rehab across the street, coordinator Pamela Fabian helps residents get ready — hair, makeup, formal clothes — because for many in full-time care, this is the only event all year they're invited to. "They never get invited to things," Fabian said. "This is so good for people who get overlooked."

stories.title: Las Vegas first responders conduct seven-hour rescue of injured climber stranded 600 feet up rock face
stories.summary: Las Vegas first responders spent seven hours rescuing an injured climber stranded 600 feet up a rock face. The complex technical rescue required specialized equipment and coordination to safely bring the climber down from the challenging height.
stories.extended_summary: Six hundred feet up a rock face, an injured climber was stranded with no way down. Las Vegas first responders launched a seven-hour technical rescue operation, carefully coordinating specialized equipment and personnel to reach the climber at a dizzying height. The rescue required precision work on vertical terrain where any mistake could prove fatal. After hours of painstaking effort, teams successfully brought the injured climber safely back to ground level.

stories.title: Students, seniors compete and connect in Olympics
stories.summary: Marblehead High School students and senior citizens competed in chair volleyball, cornhole, and curling at the first-ever Students and Seniors Olympics. Founded by three juniors after months of planning with the Council on Aging, the full-day event allowed teens and seniors to spend three hours together instead of the usual one-hour monthly meetups.
stories.extended_summary: The Community Center next to Marblehead High School was alive with bouncing volleyballs, sliding curling stones, and laughter as students and seniors competed in the first Students and Seniors Olympics. Three juniors—Sadie Halpern, Maren Rowe, and Sydney Berman—spent three months coordinating with the Council on Aging to pull off the event. Fifteen students walked to the center at 11 a.m., split into three teams with seniors, and competed fiercely in chair volleyball, cornhole, and curling. The extended format gave participants three hours together instead of the usual one-hour monthly gatherings, allowing deeper connections to form between generations through friendly competition.

stories.title: 24-YO Is on a Mission to Rescue Strays in Delhi: Your Help Can Give Them a Fighting Chance
stories.summary: At 24, Naman Sharma runs a Delhi shelter housing over 200 rescued animals and responds to up to 20 emergency calls daily. His mission started in sixth grade when he tried to save an injured pigeon—and though it didn't survive, that loss pushed him to learn animal rescue. Now his shelter needs critical medical equipment to keep saving lives.
stories.extended_summary: A sixth-grader stayed up all night trying to save an injured pigeon he found on the roadside. The bird died, but Naman Sharma didn't stop caring. Today, at 24, he runs Aashiyana by Naman & Welfare Trust in Delhi—a shelter housing over 200 rescued animals including dogs, monkeys, donkeys, and cats. His team responds to up to 20 emergency calls a day. The shelter urgently needs a veterinary CBC machine to speed up critical procedures and reduce mounting medical costs, so they can continue their round-the-clock rescue and rehabilitation work.

stories.title: Miss Vestavia Hills’ Teen fosters hope on and off stage
stories.summary: When Lyla Smith won Miss Vestavia Hills' Teen, her older sister Evie—Miss Vestavia Hills 2025—crowned her, and her younger sister Camilla became her Rising Star. Now Lyla uses her platform "Fostering Hope" to support foster children. As someone who survived childhood abuse, she knows firsthand why this work matters.
stories.extended_summary: The crown came from her sister's hands. Lyla Smith, a junior at Slocomb High School, won Miss Vestavia Hills' Teen in a moment made surreal by family: her older sister Evie, who was Miss Vestavia Hills 2025, got to crown her, and her younger sister Camilla became her Rising Star. But Lyla's platform runs deeper than pageantry. Through "Fostering Hope," she raises money and support for foster children—work rooted in her own experience surviving childhood abuse. She competes for Miss Alabama's Teen in March, carrying a mission born from pain and turned into purpose.

stories.title: From potholes to progress: neighbors fund repairs on Fuller Road
stories.summary: When Pima County said they couldn't legally maintain Fuller Road, neighbors Larry Worden and Kevin Kassner decided to fix it themselves. They challenged each other with $500 donations, knocked on doors, and raised over $5,300 from 26 donors. So far, they've filled potholes with 100 tons of material—and they're not stopping yet.
stories.extended_summary: Fuller Road in Three Points was riddled with deep holes forcing drivers to swerve just to get through. When neighbors learned the dirt portion wasn't county-maintained, Larry Worden and Kevin Kassner didn't complain—they acted. Worden's nephew-in-law challenged him with a $500 donation, sparking a grassroots fundraising effort. Kassner knocked on doors and put up signs throughout town and on Facebook. Twenty-six donors contributed over $5,300, and they've already spent $4,000 bringing in 100 tons of material to fill the worst potholes. The work continues as neighbors literally pave their own way forward.
stories.display_reason: medium image not worth the space

stories.title: Student volunteers at west side school send 20,000 meals to Haiti
stories.summary: Fifty students at Indianapolis' Northwest Middle School packed 20,000 meals for kids in Haiti—enough to feed 100 students for an entire year. The effort reflects the school's commitment to global citizenship, and many student volunteers came from Haitian families themselves, making the project deeply personal.
stories.extended_summary: Nineteen languages are spoken at Northwest Middle School in Indianapolis, and a significant number of students come from families where English is a second language. Many of Thursday's volunteers came from Haitian families, giving the meal-packing project personal weight. Around 50 students worked together to assemble 20,000 meals destined for Haiti—enough to feed 100 kids for an entire year. The project fulfills the school's International Baccalaureate standards, which require students to participate in creativity, activity, and service experiences. Assistant Principal Adriana Berry says her students are uniquely positioned to tackle global problems: "We see a problem, do something about it, fix it."

stories.title: 'You can': How Kylie LaBelle answered Detroit Lakes’ call for firefighters
stories.summary: Kylie LaBelle joined Detroit Lakes Fire Department as a volunteer firefighter after a Mississippi firefighter told her "You can" during a college service day. Now working as a biologist for U.S. Fish and Wildlife, she's helping fill critical volunteer recruitment gaps in a department struggling nationwide.
stories.extended_summary: "I wish I could do that," Kylie LaBelle told a firefighter while scrubbing engines during a Mississippi State service day. He looked at her and said two words that flipped a switch: "You can." Those words carried her from Mississippi to Detroit Lakes, where she now balances work as a U.S. Fish and Wildlife biologist with volunteer firefighting duties. She was sworn in as a full member in February — a significant win for a department facing the same volunteer shortage plaguing fire stations nationwide. LaBelle grew up hunting and fishing on family land, and that same hands-on spirit now shows up on emergency calls.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: Albuquerque teens bridge generations with GlamourGals at senior center
stories.summary: Sisters Dana and Jenna Poon started Albuquerque's first GlamourGals chapter after watching their grandmother desperately cling to their arms, not wanting them to leave. Now they bring monthly manicures and activities to local senior centers, but the real magic happens in the conversations. One regular says the girls know exactly how to ask questions that get residents talking.
stories.extended_summary: Their grandmother grabbed onto their arms as they tried to leave, desperate for them to stay. That moment made sisters Dana and Jenna Poon realize senior isolation affects more than just one person. They founded Albuquerque's first GlamourGals chapter, bringing monthly activities like painting tote bags and signature manicures to local senior centers. But residents say it's not about the polish—it's about the listening. "The girls know how to ask questions to get you to talk," one regular attendee notes. Once a month, nail polish and paint line the tables, and conversations bridge generations.

stories.title: North Dakota organization takes veterans into the outdoors, tells their stories of service and sacrifice
stories.summary: David Morse has spent over a decade telling veterans' stories through American Heroes TV Outdoors, including that of Jeff Savelkoul—a Marine whose helicopter was shot down in Vietnam at age 19, leaving him with broken bones and burns over 90% of his body. Morse says it's the stories of how veterans overcame their struggles that drive him to continue the mission.
stories.extended_summary: At 19, Jeff Savelkoul's helicopter was shot down in Vietnam. He was one of the few on his team to survive the crash, but it left him with broken bones and burns covering 90% of his body. "His life changed forever that day in Vietnam," says David Morse, choking on difficult words as he tells the story of his late friend. Morse serves as CEO and host of Fargo-based American Heroes TV Outdoors, where for over a decade he's helped tell the stories of veterans—often seeing the visual and emotional scars left from battle. "It's stories like that that make me strive to be a better person and continue our mission," Morse says, driven by the resilience of those who served.
stories.display_reason: medium image not worth the space
